name: Patecatl
description: Patecatl, in Aztec mythology, was the god of medicine, healing, and pulque, a fermented alcoholic beverage made from the maguey plant. He was often associated with Mayahuel, the goddess of the maguey plant, and the Centzon Totochtin, the four hundred rabbit gods of drunkenness. Patecatl's origins are somewhat obscure, but he is believed to have emerged from the earth itself, embodying the medicinal properties of plants and the intoxicating power of pulque. He was revered as a benevolent deity who could cure illnesses and alleviate suffering, but also as a powerful force that could lead to intoxication and altered states of consciousness. Patecatl played a crucial role in Aztec society, as medicine and pulque were both integral to their culture and religious practices. His influence extended to agriculture, as the maguey plant was a vital resource for food, fiber, and building materials, in addition to its use in pulque production. He represents the duality of nature, both healing and potentially harmful, and the interconnectedness of the natural world and human experience. His worship often involved rituals and ceremonies involving the consumption of pulque, which was believed to facilitate communication with the gods and access to divine knowledge. He is considered a vital god in the Aztec pantheon, influencing health, agriculture, and social interactions.
subtitle: God of Medicine, Healing, and Pulque

keyMyths.title: The Discovery of Pulque
keyMyths.description: Patecatl, along with Mayahuel, is credited with discovering the process of fermenting pulque from the maguey plant. This discovery brought joy and celebration to the Aztec people. The fermented beverage held significant religious and cultural importance.

keyMyths.title: Patecatl's Healing Powers
keyMyths.description: Aztec priests invoked Patecatl's name during healing rituals, believing he could bestow medicinal knowledge and restore health. Offerings of herbs and pulque were often made to him in hopes of receiving his divine assistance. He could cure illnesses and alleviate suffering.
keyMyths.source: General History of the Things of New Spain

keyMyths.title: The Four Hundred Rabbits
keyMyths.description: Patecatl is considered the father of the Centzon Totochtin, the four hundred rabbit gods of drunkenness. These gods embodied the various states of intoxication caused by pulque. These rabbit gods were born from Mayahuel.

sources.author: Bernardino de Sahagún
sources.work: General History of the Things of New Spain
sources.description: An encyclopedic work about the people and culture of central Mexico compiled by the Spanish friar Bernardino de Sahagún.
sources.text: An encyclopedic work about the people and culture of central Mexico compiled by the Spanish friar Bernardino de Sahagún.

sources.text: A historical and ethnographic account of the Aztec civilization by the Dominican friar Diego Durán.

worshipPractices.name: Herbal Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Patecatl's worship often involved offerings of medicinal herbs and plants, reflecting his role as a god of medicine. These offerings were believed to imbue the pulque with healing properties and ensure the efficacy of medical treatments.

worshipPractices.name: Pulque Rituals
worshipPractices.description: Rituals centered around the consumption of pulque were integral to Patecatl's worship. These ceremonies aimed to invoke his presence and harness his power for healing and divination. Shamans and healers often led these rituals, using pulque to enter altered states of consciousness.

festivals.name: Tecuilhuitontli
festivals.timing: The eighth month of the Aztec calendar
festivals.description: Tecuilhuitontli, meaning "Little Feast of the Lords", was dedicated to several deities including Patecatl. Celebrations involved feasting, dancing, and the consumption of pulque. Offerings were made to ensure the health and well-being of the community.

cultural.modernLegacy: While direct worship of Patecatl has ceased, his association with traditional medicine and the cultural significance of pulque persists in some regions of Mexico. There is growing interest in pre-Columbian deities, with some viewing Patecatl as a symbol of healing and ancestral wisdom.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Patecatl's origins are shrouded in the mists of Aztec mythology, with no definitive birth story widely circulated. He is generally considered to be an ancient deity, possibly predating the rise of the Aztec empire. Some accounts suggest that he emerged directly from the earth, embodying the inherent medicinal properties of the natural world. His connection to the land underscores his association with healing and the life-giving power of plants. He is often linked to Mayahuel, the goddess of the maguey plant, the source of pulque, suggesting a close relationship from the earliest stages of their mythological development. Patecatl's emergence from the earth also symbolizes the hidden knowledge of medicinal plants, waiting to be discovered and utilized by humankind.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: While Patecatl does not feature prominently in many elaborate Aztec myths, his significance lies in his association with the discovery and use of medicinal plants and the creation of pulque. He is credited with teaching humans the secrets of herbal remedies and the fermentation process that transforms the maguey sap into the sacred beverage. One prominent narrative involves Patecatl's role as the father of the Centzon Totochtin, the four hundred rabbit gods of drunkenness. This connection highlights the dual nature of pulque, both as a medicinal substance and as a source of intoxication. His association with Mayahuel reinforces the idea of a divine partnership in providing sustenance and healing to humanity. These stories, though fragmented, reveal Patecatl as a crucial figure in the Aztec understanding of health, intoxication, and the natural world.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Patecatl's worship was closely intertwined with the practice of medicine and the consumption of pulque. He was venerated by healers, shamans, and anyone seeking relief from illness or injury. Offerings of medicinal herbs, plants, and pulque were made to invoke his presence and seek his blessings. Rituals often involved the use of pulque as a sacred beverage, believed to possess healing properties and facilitate communication with the divine. During the festival of Tecuilhuitontli, Patecatl was honored alongside other agricultural deities, reflecting his connection to the earth and its bounty. Images of Patecatl typically depicted him adorned with medicinal plants and carrying tools used in healing practices. His cult was likely widespread throughout the Aztec empire, wherever pulque was produced and consumed for medicinal or ritual purposes.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Though the Aztec empire fell to Spanish conquistadors, the legacy of Patecatl continues to resonate in contemporary Mexican culture. His association with traditional medicine and the cultural significance of pulque has ensured his enduring presence in the collective memory. In some rural communities, traditional healers still draw upon the knowledge of medicinal plants passed down through generations, indirectly honoring Patecatl's role as the divine source of this wisdom. The resurgence of interest in pre-Columbian deities has also led to a renewed appreciation for Patecatl as a symbol of healing, ancestral knowledge, and the interconnectedness of humans and nature. While he is no longer actively worshipped, Patecatl remains a powerful reminder of the rich cultural heritage of the Aztec civilization and the enduring importance of traditional medicine.

shortDescription: Aztec god of medicine, healing, and pulque. Embodied medicinal properties of the earth and taught humans herbal remedies and fermentation.
